growthHint.title: Growth company detected
growthHint.body: Companies like Amazon or Tesla reinvest most profits, making EPS very low. Analysts typically use Free Cash Flow per share (FCF/share) instead of EPS for more accurate valuation. You can find FCF/share on financial sites like Macrotrends or Simply Wall St.

fairPrice.epsTooltip: Use EPS for stable companies. For high-growth companies, compare this result with revenue, margins and cash flow before trusting it.

financial.title: Bank or fintech profile detected
financial.text: A pure EPS DCF can be too blunt for banks, lenders and fintechs. Revenue growth, loan quality, deposits, ROE and operating leverage can matter more than today’s EPS, so Lucrum switches to a multiple scenario when enough data is available.

growth.title: Growth profile detected
growth.text: Current EPS can understate companies that reinvest heavily. Treat the DCF as a conservative stress test and compare it with a P/E or P/S scenario.

lowEps.title: Low EPS signal
lowEps.text: When EPS is small relative to price, an EPS DCF can look harsh even if the market is pricing future margin expansion. Use a multiple scenario to separate business growth from today’s profit base.

modeInfo.title: {{mode}} active
modeInfo.dcf: Best for profitable, stable businesses where EPS already reflects the business engine. It is intentionally conservative for early growth stories.
modeInfo.pe: Useful when you believe the company will deserve a specific earnings multiple. Fair price = EPS × target P/E.
modeInfo.ps: Useful for growth, fintech and low-margin companies where revenue scale matters before mature earnings. Fair price = revenue per share × target P/S.

unavailable.title: Model needs more data
unavailable.text: Enter {{fields}} to calculate this model. Missing live data is not treated as zero.

explain.marketPrice: The price the market is currently showing.
explain.upside: Distance between market price and the model's safe-buy price.
explain.forecastPv: Present value of the first five forecast years.
explain.terminalPv: Present value of the value after year five.
explain.currentPe: How many dollars investors pay for one dollar of earnings today.
explain.targetPe: The earnings multiple you assume could be fair.
explain.currentPs: How many dollars investors pay for one dollar of sales today.
explain.targetPs: The sales multiple you assume could be fair.

verdict.undervaluedText: The market price is below the safety-adjusted buy price.
verdict.overvaluedText: The market price is above the safety-adjusted buy price.

formula.title: Formula
formula.text: Sum PV (years 1-5) + Terminal PV = Fair Price.
formula.dcf: Sum PV (years 1-5) + Terminal PV = Fair Price.
formula.pe: EPS (TTM) × target P/E = Fair Price. Safety-adjusted buy price then subtracts the margin of safety.
formula.ps: Revenue per share × target P/S = Fair Price. Safety-adjusted buy price then subtracts the margin of safety.

how.title: How it works
how.text: Think of a company like an apple tree. EPS is the apples it produces today, growth estimates more apples in the next five years, and the discount rate asks what those future apples are worth in today's money.
how.dcf: Think of a company like an apple tree. EPS is the apples it produces today, growth estimates more apples in the next five years, and the discount rate asks what those future apples are worth in today's money.
how.pe: Choose the earnings multiple you think the market could reasonably pay. This is simpler than DCF and makes the assumption visible: the result depends on both EPS quality and the target P/E.
how.ps: Choose the sales multiple you think the business could deserve. This can fit companies where revenue scale is clear but profits are still ramping, but it must be checked against margins and cash flow.

beginner.title: What do these numbers mean?
beginner.description: This calculator does not tell you to buy or sell. It shows the price implied by your selected model and how far the market is from that price.

fairPrice.title: Fair price
fairPrice.text: An estimated share price from the selected model. It is not a forecast or guarantee, just the output of your assumptions.

safeBuy.title: Safe buy price
safeBuy.text: Fair price minus the margin of safety. It is lower so there is room for errors in the data or forecast.

margin.title: Margin of safety
margin.text: A discount to the model price. 30% means you only consider buying if the market is roughly one-third below your estimate.

eps.title: EPS
eps.text: Earnings per share. If a company earns money steadily, EPS can be a useful base for valuation.

pe.title: P/E
pe.text: How much the market pays for 1 dollar of earnings. A higher P/E means the market expects more from the future.

ps.title: P/S
ps.text: How much the market pays for 1 dollar of revenue. It can help with companies whose profits are still uneven.

dcf.title: DCF
dcf.text: An attempt to estimate what future profits are worth today. Bolder assumptions create bolder results.

discount.title: Discount rate
discount.text: The higher it is, the stricter the model is about future money. It is a way to account for time and risk.

steps.title: How to use it without a finance degree
steps.chooseModel: Start with a model you understand: DCF or P/E for stable profitable companies, often P/S for fintech and growth companies.
steps.checkInputs: Check the input numbers. If EPS, revenue, or price looks strange, the result will be strange too.
steps.comparePrices: Compare the market price with the safe buy price, not only with the fair price.
steps.useCaution: If upside is negative, it is not an order to sell. It means the price is high under your current assumptions.

warning.title: When not to trust the result
warning.text: Do not rely on one model for banks, unprofitable growth companies, cyclical businesses, or tickers with incomplete live data. Compare several scenarios and understand the business, not only the number.

cagr.definition: Compound annual growth rate. It shows the smoothed yearly growth rate over a period.

eps.definition: Earnings per share. It tells you how much profit belongs to one share.

pe.definition: Price divided by earnings. It is a quick way to compare price with current profits.

fcf.definition: Cash left after a company pays for operations and necessary investments.

margin.definition: A buffer between your estimated value and the price you are willing to pay.

faq.description: Quick answers before you open a calculator.

start.question: Where should I start?
start.answer: Start with your goal. If you want long-term growth, use Invest Calc. If you want financial independence, use FIRE. If you want to test monthly investing, use DCA.

dca.question: Is DCA better than investing all at once?
dca.answer: Not always. Lump-sum investing can win when markets rise quickly, but DCA is easier emotionally and reduces the risk of investing everything right before a drop.

fire.question: What is FIRE?
fire.answer: FIRE means Financial Independence, Retire Early. The basic idea is to build a portfolio large enough to cover expenses through withdrawals.

fairPrice.question: What is a fair price?
fairPrice.answer: A fair price is an estimate of what a business might be worth today based on future earnings, growth, and risk. It is an estimate, not a precise truth.

risk.question: What is margin of safety?
risk.answer: Margin of safety means buying below your estimated fair value so small mistakes in your assumptions do not immediately break the investment case.

data.question: Are calculator results financial advice?
data.answer: No. They are educational planning tools. Use them to understand scenarios, then do deeper research before investing.

miniCourse.title: 17 steps before your first stock
miniCourse.description: A full original 17-part beginner path through financial foundations, risk profile, asset choice, ETFs, business analysis, statements, valuation, entry methods, and technical indicators.
miniCourse.previewOneText: The course starts with personal finances because even a good stock cannot fix a weak plan.
miniCourse.previewTwoText: Each lesson includes a practical situation: how to think, what to check, and where beginners often slip.
miniCourse.previewThreeText: The stocks topic stays compact, while the deeper material opens as its own learning path.
miniCourse.ruleText: Do not buy a stock only because it is popular or has dropped. First understand the business, price, risk, time horizon, and the role this position has in your portfolio.
miniCourse.finalText: After the course you should have a short investment note: why this company, how it makes money, what can go wrong, what valuation you accept, what your horizon is, and what would make you change your mind. If you cannot write that note in simple words, it is probably too early to buy.

bonds.title: Bonds
bonds.summary: Loans to governments or companies that usually pay interest and return principal at maturity.
bonds.why: Useful for income, planning known dates, and reducing portfolio volatility, but not risk-free.
bonds.intro: A bond is a loan. You lend money to a government, city, or company. In return, the issuer usually pays interest and promises to repay the principal on a maturity date.

sections.what: Bonds are debt securities. The buyer becomes a lender, not an owner. The bond contract defines the coupon, maturity, and repayment priority.
sections.earn: Returns usually come from coupon payments, repayment at maturity, and price changes if rates move after you buy.
sections.risks: Bond prices can fall when interest rates rise. Issuers can also default, and inflation can reduce the real value of fixed payments.
sections.mistakes: A high yield is not automatically a bargain. It can mean the market sees higher credit or duration risk.
sections.fit: Bonds may fit money you want to stabilize, income needs, or goals with a clearer date than long-term stock growth.

risks.rates: Interest-rate risk: existing bond prices often fall when new bonds offer higher yields.
risks.credit: Credit risk: the issuer may struggle to pay interest or repay principal.
risks.inflation: Inflation risk: fixed payments can buy less in the future.

mistakes.yieldOnly: Choosing only the highest yield without asking why the yield is high.
mistakes.duration: Ignoring duration and being surprised when a bond fund falls after rates rise.
mistakes.cash: Treating bonds like cash even though market prices can move.

fit.income: You want more predictable interest income.
fit.stability: You want part of the portfolio to move differently from stocks.
fit.knownDate: You are matching money to a future date, like tuition or a home deposit.

coupon.definition: The stated interest payment a bond pays, usually shown as an annual percentage of face value.

yield.definition: The return implied by the bond price, coupon, and time to maturity.

maturity.definition: The date when the issuer is expected to repay the bond principal.

duration.definition: A measure of how sensitive a bond or bond fund is to interest-rate changes.

quiz.question: If you buy a bond, what role do you take?
options: Lender to the issuer
Owner of the issuer
Manager of the issuer
quiz.explanation: A bond is debt. The buyer lends money and expects interest plus repayment at maturity.

quiz.question: What usually happens to existing bond prices when market rates rise sharply?
options: They often fall
They always rise
They become identical to cash
quiz.explanation: New bonds may offer higher yields, so older lower-yield bonds usually need a lower price to compete.

quiz.question: Why can a high bond yield be a warning?
options: It may reflect higher credit or duration risk
It guarantees a bargain
It removes default risk
quiz.explanation: Yield is compensation for risk. A very high yield often means the market is worried about repayment, rates, or liquidity.

quiz.question: Which term measures sensitivity to interest-rate changes?
options: Duration
Coupon
Maturity date only
quiz.explanation: Duration estimates how much a bond or bond fund may move when rates change.

quiz.question: When can bonds fit better than stocks?
options: For income or a goal with a clearer date
For money you want to double quickly
For avoiding all inflation risk
quiz.explanation: Bonds can help stabilize part of a portfolio or match known future cash needs.

quiz.question: A user needs tuition money in two years. What is the better bond lesson?
options: Avoid treating long-duration bond funds like cash
Buy only the highest-yield bond
Ignore issuer quality
quiz.explanation: A near-term goal needs stability. Long duration can still fall when rates rise.

stocks.title: Stocks
stocks.summary: Ownership stakes in companies, with returns driven by business performance, expectations, and dividends.
stocks.why: Stocks can build long-term wealth, but the price can move far more than the company changes in a single year.
stocks.intro: A share of stock represents partial ownership in a company. If the business grows, earns more, and the market values it well, shareholders can benefit.

sections.what: Stocks are equity. You own a small claim on a business, not a fixed promise of repayment.
sections.earn: Returns can come from price appreciation, dividends, buybacks, and growth in earnings or cash flow.
sections.risks: A company can disappoint, get disrupted, take on too much debt, or simply become too expensive relative to its profits.
sections.mistakes: A familiar brand is not automatically a good investment. The price you pay still matters.
sections.fit: Stocks may fit long-term growth goals where you can tolerate volatility and research business quality.

foundation.title: Prepare your financial base
foundation.text: Before stocks, you need an emergency fund, debt control, and a clear view of monthly cash flow. Otherwise the market can force you to sell at the worst time.
foundation.details: Stocks move around. Even a strong company can fall 20-40% because of the market, rates, an earnings report, or investor fear. If you do not have a reserve, an ordinary life problem can force you to sell for cash instead of because the investment thesis changed. The financial base is not boring paperwork; it protects you from forced decisions. Practical anchor: Keep 3-6 months of expenses outside stocks before investing aggressively.
foundation.example: Imagine someone invests $1,000 in a stock and two months later loses part of their income or needs to replace a laptop. Without a reserve, they may sell at a loss even if the business is still healthy. Someone with a reserve can separate a life problem from an investing decision. Practical anchor: Keep 3-6 months of expenses outside stocks before investing aggressively.
foundation.caseStudy: Mia earns $1,800 a month and spends about $1,200. She wants to invest all $3,000 of savings at once because she fears missing the next rally. A steadier path is to keep 3-6 months of expenses as a reserve, remove expensive debt, and invest from future monthly cash flow. Then stocks do not compete with basic safety. Decision check: Investing money needed soon can force a sale during a normal 20-40% decline.
foundation.action: Build a reserve for 3-6 months of expenses, list high-interest debts, and calculate the amount you can realistically invest each month.
foundation.checkOne: The emergency reserve is not stored in stocks.
foundation.checkTwo: High-interest debt is not eating more than investments can reasonably earn.
foundation.checkThree: The monthly investing amount does not break the budget or create stress.
foundation.warning: Do not invest money that may be needed soon for rent, healthcare, education, taxes, or other required payments.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: A reserve and debt control come before stock selection.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: A reserve and debt control come before stock selection. The practical anchor is: Keep 3-6 months of expenses outside stocks before investing aggressively. The main trap is: Investing money needed soon can force a sale during a normal 20-40% decline.

quiz.question: Which practical check fits this lesson best?
options: Keep 3-6 months of expenses outside stocks before investing aggressively.
Assume price movement alone explains the whole investment.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: A reserve and debt control come before stock selection. The practical anchor is: Keep 3-6 months of expenses outside stocks before investing aggressively. The main trap is: Investing money needed soon can force a sale during a normal 20-40% decline.

quiz.question: What mistake should a beginner avoid here?
options: Investing money needed soon can force a sale during a normal 20-40% decline.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Assume price movement alone explains the whole investment.
quiz.explanation: A reserve and debt control come before stock selection. The practical anchor is: Keep 3-6 months of expenses outside stocks before investing aggressively. The main trap is: Investing money needed soon can force a sale during a normal 20-40% decline.

quiz.question: In the example, what happens to someone without a reserve who invested $1,000 and then loses part of their income two months later?
options: They may be forced to sell the stock at a loss even though the business is still healthy
Their broker automatically pauses the investment until income returns
The $1,000 is protected by law from being withdrawn during a hardship
quiz.explanation: Without a reserve, a normal life problem can force a sale at a bad time, even if nothing changed about the company itself. That is exactly the forced-decision risk this lesson warns about.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson call the financial base 'not boring paperwork' but something that protects you from forced decisions?
options: Because a reserve lets you ride out a 20-40% stock decline without needing to sell for cash
Because paperwork is required by brokers before you can open an account
Because it guarantees the stock you bought will recover within 3-6 months
quiz.explanation: A reserve separates ordinary life expenses from your investment decisions, so a market drop does not turn into a forced sale. It is about behavior under stress, not about paperwork or guarantees.

quiz.question: Daniel has no emergency reserve but feels confident because he reads about the market every day. His car needs an unexpected $900 repair right after a 25% dip in his only stock holding. What should he do, based on this lesson's principle?
options: Sell part of the stock to cover the repair, since he has no other reserve to draw from
Ignore the repair and let the bill go unpaid until the stock price recovers
Take on high-interest debt for the repair so the stock position stays untouched
quiz.explanation: Without a reserve, Daniel is left with no good options. Confidence and research do not substitute for cash set aside, which is precisely why the lesson stresses building a reserve before investing aggressively.

quiz.question: According to the lesson's warning, which of these is the clearest example of money that should NOT be invested in stocks?
options: Funds set aside for next quarter's tax payment
Money from a monthly budget surplus after all expenses and debt payments
Savings specifically earmarked for retirement in 25 years
quiz.explanation: The warning specifically lists rent, healthcare, education, and taxes as near-term obligations that should never be exposed to stock market volatility. Long-horizon savings and true surplus cash are different from money needed soon for required payments.

plan.title: Write a personal plan
plan.text: An investing plan answers three questions: why the money is invested, when it is needed, and how much drawdown you can realistically tolerate.
plan.details: Without a plan, an investor keeps reacting to noise: news, chats, red days, and other people's gains. A plan should define the goal, horizon, contribution rhythm, maximum size of one stock, and selling rules. It does not guarantee profit, but it reduces chaos. A good plan is short enough to read in a minute when the market feels emotional. Practical anchor: Set a horizon, monthly contribution, and maximum single-stock weight such as 5-8%.
plan.example: A plan can say: I invest for 10 years, add $300 each month, keep one stock below 8% of the portfolio, buy only profitable and understandable businesses, and sell because the thesis breaks rather than because the price fell. Practical anchor: Set a horizon, monthly contribution, and maximum single-stock weight such as 5-8%.
plan.caseStudy: Owen bought three popular stocks after a video. One dropped 18%, and he did not know whether to buy more, sell, or wait. With a plan, the decision would be clearer: position below 5%, add only after checking the report, sell only if revenue weakens or the company loses its edge. Decision check: Changing strategy after every video is not discipline.
plan.action: Write your goal, horizon, recurring contribution, maximum position size, and selling rule.
plan.checkOne: The goal has a horizon, not just the phrase make money.
plan.checkTwo: Each position has a reason beyond a good-looking chart.
plan.checkThree: The selling rule is written before the purchase.
plan.warning: A plan that changes after every video or post is not a plan. It is an emotional reaction with a better name.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: A written plan turns market noise into pre-made rules.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: A written plan turns market noise into pre-made rules. The practical anchor is: Set a horizon, monthly contribution, and maximum single-stock weight such as 5-8%. The main trap is: Changing strategy after every video is not discipline.

quiz.question: Which practical check fits this lesson best?
options: Set a horizon, monthly contribution, and maximum single-stock weight such as 5-8%.
Assume price movement alone explains the whole investment.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: A written plan turns market noise into pre-made rules. The practical anchor is: Set a horizon, monthly contribution, and maximum single-stock weight such as 5-8%. The main trap is: Changing strategy after every video is not discipline.

quiz.question: What mistake should a beginner avoid here?
options: Changing strategy after every video is not discipline.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Assume price movement alone explains the whole investment.
quiz.explanation: A written plan turns market noise into pre-made rules. The practical anchor is: Set a horizon, monthly contribution, and maximum single-stock weight such as 5-8%. The main trap is: Changing strategy after every video is not discipline.

quiz.question: In the example plan, what maximum weight does the investor set for a single stock?
options: 8% of the portfolio
25% of the portfolio
50% of the portfolio
quiz.explanation: The example plan explicitly caps any single stock below 8% of the portfolio, which keeps one disappointing company from doing major damage to the whole plan.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson say a plan should be 'short enough to read in a minute when the market feels emotional'?
options: Because during stressful moments, a quick, clear reference helps you act on prior logic instead of panic
Because longer plans are against brokerage regulations
Because a one-minute plan is statistically proven to outperform longer ones
quiz.explanation: The point of brevity is usability under stress: when emotions run high, you need to quickly recall your own rules rather than reconstruct your reasoning from scratch.

quiz.question: Priya buys a stock after a friend's tip, then sees a bearish video the next day and considers selling immediately, then sees a bullish post and considers buying more. What does this lesson suggest she's missing?
options: A written plan with selling rules and position limits that don't change with each new piece of content she sees
A faster phone so she can react to news before other investors do
A larger position size so small price moves matter less emotionally
quiz.explanation: Reacting to each new video or post is the emotional pattern the lesson explicitly warns against. A written plan with a fixed selling rule would let Priya ignore the noise and follow a thesis instead.

quiz.question: Based on checkThree, when should your selling rule for a stock be decided?
options: Before you make the purchase
After the first earnings report following purchase
Only once the stock has fallen by double digits
quiz.explanation: Deciding the selling rule before buying ensures the decision is made with a clear head, not under the pressure of an actual price move. Writing it down afterward usually means it's being shaped by emotion instead of logic.

truthsMyths.title: Truths and myths about investing
truthsMyths.text: Investing is not a quick-rich button. It is a system of decisions where patience, risk control, and entry price matter more than loud promises.
truthsMyths.details: The sweet myth says you only need to find one stock that will make you rich. The harder truth is less glamorous: most results come from discipline, diversification, risk control, a long horizon, and the ability to keep a plan when the market makes you look wrong. The market does not pay for emotions, ticker popularity, or confidence in a chat. It rewards buying quality assets at reasonable prices and surviving uncomfortable periods. Practical anchor: A 30% fall does not automatically make a stock cheap if profits fall too.
truthsMyths.example: A stock can rise 80% in one year and still be a poor buy today if the price already assumes a perfect scenario. A broad ETF may look less exciting, but it can be a better fit for someone building capital over 10-20 years. Practical anchor: A 30% fall does not automatically make a stock cheap if profits fall too.
truthsMyths.caseStudy: Den sees a story about someone buying the bottom and making 5x. He does not see the many similar stories that ended badly. Survivorship bias makes investing look easier than it is. A healthy process asks not only how much can I make, but what happens if the outcome is average or bad. Decision check: Confusing a lower price with lower risk leads to weak decisions.
truthsMyths.action: Write down three investment promises that sound too good, then write the hidden risk next to each one.
truthsMyths.checkOne: You can separate investing from speculation and gambling.
truthsMyths.checkTwo: Expected return is connected to risk, not invented from hope.
truthsMyths.checkThree: Your plan is not built on someone else's success story without looking at losses.
truthsMyths.warning: If an idea is sold as easy money without risk, the risk is usually just unnamed.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: Common market myths must be checked against evidence.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: Common market myths must be checked against evidence. The practical anchor is: A 30% fall does not automatically make a stock cheap if profits fall too. The main trap is: Confusing a lower price with lower risk leads to weak decisions.

options: A 30% fall does not automatically make a stock cheap if profits fall too.

options: Confusing a lower price with lower risk leads to weak decisions.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Assume price movement alone explains the whole investment.
quiz.explanation: Common market myths must be checked against evidence. The practical anchor is: A 30% fall does not automatically make a stock cheap if profits fall too. The main trap is: Confusing a lower price with lower risk leads to weak decisions.

quiz.question: In the lesson's example, what is true about a stock that rose 80% in one year?
options: It can still be a poor buy today if the price already assumes a perfect scenario
It is automatically a safer investment than it was a year ago
It cannot fall further because it has already proven strong demand
quiz.explanation: Past performance does not tell you what is already priced in. A stock that already assumes a perfect future can be a poor buy today regardless of how well it performed previously.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson mention survivorship bias in Den's case study?
options: Because hearing only about big winners hides the many similar attempts that ended badly, making big bets look safer than they are
Because Den's broker only shows him stocks that survived a stock split
Because survivorship bias means old companies always outperform new ones
quiz.explanation: Survivorship bias distorts perception by spotlighting winners and ignoring failures, which makes risky bets look more reliable than the real odds suggest.

quiz.question: Lucia hears that her coworker turned $2,000 into $11,000 buying a single volatile stock, and decides to put her entire savings into a similar trade. What does this lesson suggest she consider first?
options: That she is only hearing one success story and not the likely range of outcomes, including losses, behind similar trades
That she should ask the coworker for the exact ticker symbol to copy the trade exactly
That doubling her position size would have doubled the coworker's result too
quiz.explanation: One anecdote is not evidence of typical results. The lesson stresses asking what happens in an average or bad outcome, not just chasing a story about an exceptional one.

quiz.question: Per the lesson's warning, what should you assume when an investment idea is marketed as 'easy money without risk'?
options: The risk is real but simply not being named
The opportunity is regulated and therefore inherently safe
It must be a long-term, low-volatility investment like a bond
quiz.explanation: The lesson is direct on this point: claims of easy, riskless money almost always have a hidden risk that has not been disclosed, not an actual absence of risk.

riskProfile.title: Define your risk profile
riskProfile.text: Risk is not only price decline. It also includes income shocks, panic, concentration, currency, sector exposure, and mistakes in your assumptions.
riskProfile.details: A risk profile is not about how brave you sound. It is about how you behave when the portfolio really falls. For stocks, think about volatility, concentration, currency exposure, sector dependence, and emotional comfort. Beginners often overestimate their tolerance during good markets and discover their real risk level only in a drawdown. Practical anchor: Money needed in under 3 years should not rely on volatile stocks.
riskProfile.example: Two people may have the same amount of money but different risk profiles. One has stable income, a reserve, and a 15-year horizon. The other needs a home deposit next year. The same market drop may be tolerable for the first person and damaging for the second. Practical anchor: Money needed in under 3 years should not rely on volatile stocks.
riskProfile.caseStudy: Ira's portfolio is 80% technology stocks. On paper there are many tickers, but the risk is similar: rates, growth valuations, and sentiment toward tech. If the sector falls, almost everything falls together. Her real risk is higher than the number of companies suggests. Decision check: Taking equity risk without time to recover creates forced selling.
riskProfile.action: Set maximum weights for one stock, one sector, and all risky assets in your portfolio.
riskProfile.checkOne: One stock cannot damage the whole plan by itself.
riskProfile.checkTwo: Different tickers are not all exposed to the same sector risk.
riskProfile.checkThree: You know what you would do if the portfolio fell 20%, 30%, or 40%.
riskProfile.warning: If a 10% fall keeps you awake, do not build a portfolio as if a 50% fall would be easy.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: Risk tolerance must match time horizon and income stability.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: Risk tolerance must match time horizon and income stability. The practical anchor is: Money needed in under 3 years should not rely on volatile stocks. The main trap is: Taking equity risk without time to recover creates forced selling.

quiz.question: Which practical check fits this lesson best?
options: Money needed in under 3 years should not rely on volatile stocks.
Assume price movement alone explains the whole investment.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: Risk tolerance must match time horizon and income stability. The practical anchor is: Money needed in under 3 years should not rely on volatile stocks. The main trap is: Taking equity risk without time to recover creates forced selling.

quiz.question: What mistake should a beginner avoid here?
options: Taking equity risk without time to recover creates forced selling.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Assume price movement alone explains the whole investment.
quiz.explanation: Risk tolerance must match time horizon and income stability. The practical anchor is: Money needed in under 3 years should not rely on volatile stocks. The main trap is: Taking equity risk without time to recover creates forced selling.

quiz.question: In the example, why might the same market drop be tolerable for one person but damaging for another?
options: Because one person has a stable income, reserve, and 15-year horizon, while the other needs a home deposit next year
Because one person owns more shares in absolute number than the other
Because risk tolerance is fixed by age and cannot differ between people with similar timelines
quiz.explanation: Risk profile depends on circumstances like time horizon, income stability, and near-term cash needs, not just on the dollar amount invested. The same drop affects each person differently based on those factors.

quiz.question: Why does Ira's case study say her real risk is 'higher than the number of companies suggests' even though her portfolio holds many tickers?
options: Because 80% of her holdings are concentrated in technology, so they tend to fall together from the same shared risks
Because owning many tickers always increases trading fees and therefore risk
Because technology stocks are inherently riskier than every other sector at all times
quiz.explanation: Owning many tickers in the same sector does not create real diversification, since they're all exposed to the same rate, valuation, and sentiment risks. True diversification requires spreading across uncorrelated exposures.

quiz.question: Tom needs $15,000 for a home deposit in 14 months, but currently holds that amount in a single volatile growth stock because he wants higher returns first. What does this lesson suggest about his approach?
options: His risk profile is mismatched with his timeline, since money needed soon should not rely on volatile stocks
His approach is fine because growth stocks recover within any 14-month window historically
His approach is acceptable as long as he diversifies into two stocks instead of one
quiz.explanation: The lesson's practical anchor states that money needed in under 3 years should not rely on volatile stocks. Tom's short timeline makes this a mismatch regardless of how many stocks he holds.

quiz.question: The lesson's warning says if a 10% fall keeps you awake at night, what should you avoid doing?
options: Building a portfolio as if a 50% fall would be easy to handle
Checking your portfolio balance more than once a week
Investing in any asset class other than bonds
quiz.explanation: The warning highlights a mismatch between emotional tolerance and portfolio construction. If small drops already cause distress, a portfolio built for large potential drawdowns is not aligned with the investor's real risk profile.

assetMap.title: Compare asset classes
assetMap.text: Stocks give business ownership, bonds behave more like loans, ETFs give a basket, and cash gives flexibility. Each tool has a different job.
assetMap.details: One stock should not replace the whole investing toolkit. Stocks can provide growth, but they charge for it with volatility. Bonds can stabilize part of the portfolio. ETFs offer broad diversification. Cash or short-term instruments provide flexibility. When you understand each asset's job, you are less likely to expect the wrong thing from it. Practical anchor: A simple starter map can cap one stock at 5% and keep cash for near-term needs.
assetMap.example: If your goal is long-term growth, stocks or ETFs can be the core. If your goal is 18 months away, such as a home deposit, a volatile single stock may be the wrong tool even if you like the company. Practical anchor: A simple starter map can cap one stock at 5% and keep cash for near-term needs.
assetMap.caseStudy: Andrew has $10,000 and wants to learn stock picking without turning the whole portfolio into a bet. One structure could be 70% broad ETF, 20% bonds or cash, and 10% individual stocks. He gets practice, but the result does not depend on a few decisions. Decision check: Owning ten companies from one sector is still concentrated risk.
assetMap.action: Decide whether stocks are your portfolio core, a small active sleeve, or a learning position.
assetMap.checkOne: Short-term goals are not funded with assets that can fall sharply before the spending date.
assetMap.checkTwo: The portfolio has something for stability, not only for growth.
assetMap.checkThree: Individual stocks have a defined role instead of being bought out of boredom.
assetMap.warning: Do not call a risky bet an investment only because it was made through a brokerage app.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: Diversification means assigning jobs to several asset types.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: Diversification means assigning jobs to several asset types. The practical anchor is: A simple starter map can cap one stock at 5% and keep cash for near-term needs. The main trap is: Owning ten companies from one sector is still concentrated risk.

quiz.question: In Andrew's case study, what portfolio structure is suggested for his $10,000 so he can learn stock picking without making the whole portfolio a bet?
options: 70% broad ETF, 20% bonds or cash, and 10% individual stocks
100% individual stocks split across ten companies
50% cash and 50% individual stocks for balance
quiz.explanation: This structure gives Andrew a stable core (the ETF and bonds/cash) while still letting him practice stock picking with a limited, contained portion of his money.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson argue that a volatile single stock may be the 'wrong tool' for a goal that is 18 months away, even if you like the company?
options: Because the short timeline doesn't allow enough time to recover from a sharp price decline before the money is needed
Because brokers restrict selling individual stocks within 18 months of purchase
Because liking a company has no bearing on whether it is a quality business
quiz.explanation: Asset choice should match the goal's timeline. A volatile holding might not recover in time if you need the cash on a fixed near-term date, regardless of how much you believe in the company.

quiz.question: Wei has $8,000 and wants to buy individual shares in ten different airline companies, believing this counts as full diversification. Based on this lesson, what is the issue?
options: Owning ten companies from one sector is still concentrated risk, not true diversification
Ten companies is too few to track, so he should reduce it to three
Airlines are the only sector where this kind of concentration is actually safe
quiz.explanation: The lesson's decision check explicitly states that owning ten companies from one sector is still concentrated risk. Real diversification requires spreading across different sectors and asset classes, not just adding more tickers within the same one.

quiz.question: According to the lesson's warning, what mistake do people make when they call a risky bet an 'investment'?
options: They assume it qualifies as an investment simply because it was made through a brokerage app
They assume it qualifies as an investment only if it loses money
They assume it qualifies as an investment only if held for over 10 years
quiz.explanation: The lesson warns that using a normal brokerage app does not change the nature of a risky, speculative bet. The platform used has no bearing on whether the underlying decision is sound investing or speculation.

compound.title: Understand compounding
compound.text: Long-term returns come from time, reinvestment, and consistency. Large outcomes often look boring at the beginning.
compound.details: Compounding is not magic; it is repetition. Returns start earning future returns. The hard part is that the first years can look slow, so beginners often abandon a systematic strategy and chase a quick jump. Time, regular contributions, dividend reinvestment, and fee control often matter more than one brilliant purchase. Practical anchor: At 8% annual growth, $1,000 can become about $4,660 in 20 years before taxes and fees.
compound.example: When you invest the same amount every month, early results depend more on contributions than returns. Later, the accumulated base starts moving the portfolio more strongly. That is why horizon and discipline matter so much. Practical anchor: At 8% annual growth, $1,000 can become about $4,660 in 20 years before taxes and fees.
compound.caseStudy: Two investors start differently. The first invests $200 every month for 15 years. The second waits five years for the perfect moment and then invests more. Often the first has the edge not because of genius, but because of time in the market. Compounding likes early starts and repetition. Decision check: Interrupting compounding too often weakens the main engine.
compound.action: Compare 5, 10, and 20 years in the investment calculator with different contributions and expected returns.
compound.checkOne: Dividends and gains have a reinvestment rule.
compound.checkTwo: Expected return does not replace a realistic contribution plan.
compound.checkThree: Fees, taxes, and currency are at least roughly considered.
compound.warning: Do not use unrealistic 30-50% annual returns as your base case. They can make a weak idea look beautiful on a screen.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: Compounding works when gains stay invested long enough.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: Compounding works when gains stay invested long enough. The practical anchor is: At 8% annual growth, $1,000 can become about $4,660 in 20 years before taxes and fees. The main trap is: Interrupting compounding too often weakens the main engine.

options: Interrupting compounding too often weakens the main engine.

quiz.question: According to the lesson's practical anchor, what can $1,000 become after 20 years at 8% annual growth, before taxes and fees?
options: About $4,660
About $2,000
About $10,000
quiz.explanation: The lesson states this figure directly as its practical anchor, illustrating how compounding multiplies an initial sum nearly fivefold over two decades at a steady 8% rate.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson say early years of a monthly investing plan 'depend more on contributions than returns'?
options: Because the invested base is still small, so the new money added each month outweighs the growth generated by the existing balance
Because returns are mathematically guaranteed to be zero in the first few years
Because brokers charge higher fees during the first years of an account
quiz.explanation: When the portfolio balance is small, the dollar amount you contribute matters more than the percentage return on a small base. As the base grows over years, the return itself starts contributing more.

quiz.question: Two friends each plan to invest $150 per month. One starts immediately; the other waits two years to find the 'perfect entry point' before starting. Based on this lesson, what is the likely outcome?
options: The one who started immediately likely has an edge from extra time in the market, not from picking a better moment
The one who waited will likely end up ahead because they avoided early volatility
Both will end up with identical results since the total contributed amount is what matters most
quiz.explanation: The case study makes this exact point: time in the market often beats waiting for a perfect moment, because compounding rewards early, consistent starts rather than precise timing.

quiz.question: Per the lesson's warning, why is it risky to use a 30-50% annual return as your base-case assumption?
options: It can make a weak investment idea look beautiful on a screen, since unrealistic growth rates inflate projected outcomes
It is illegal to model returns above 10% per year in most calculators
It guarantees a tax penalty if the assumption is later proven wrong
quiz.explanation: The warning is about distorted expectations: plugging in unrealistic return assumptions can make almost any plan or idea appear far more attractive than it would with a realistic, sustainable growth rate.

analysisTypes.title: Separate fundamental and technical analysis
analysisTypes.text: Fundamental analysis asks what the business is and what it may be worth. Technical analysis focuses more on price, trend, and market behavior.
analysisTypes.details: Fundamental analysis starts with the business: what it sells, to whom, at what margin, how it grows, how much debt it carries, and what price the market asks for it. Technical analysis may help with timing or sentiment, but it does not tell you whether the business is good. For a long-term investor, a chart without business understanding becomes guesswork. Practical anchor: Use fundamentals for business quality and technicals mainly for timing or risk control.
analysisTypes.example: A stock may break out to a new high while margins are falling and debt is expensive. Another stock may look weak after a broad market selloff while the company still produces steady cash flow and keeps its competitive advantage. Practical anchor: Use fundamentals for business quality and technicals mainly for timing or risk control.
analysisTypes.caseStudy: Peter buys because a stock breaks out of a range. A week later the company reports slower revenue, lower margins, and weaker guidance. The chart showed price behavior, but it did not replace business analysis. For an investor, those are different jobs. Decision check: A chart pattern cannot replace understanding revenue, margins, and cash flow.
analysisTypes.action: Explain the business and profit sources first, then look at the chart and entry point.
analysisTypes.checkOne: You can describe the business without opening the price chart.
analysisTypes.checkTwo: A technical signal is not the only reason to buy.
analysisTypes.checkThree: The fundamental thesis has metrics you can monitor.
analysisTypes.warning: Do not use a technical signal as an excuse to buy a company you do not understand.

Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.

quiz.question: In the lesson's example, what can happen to a stock that breaks out to a new high?
options: It can break out even while margins are falling and debt is getting more expensive
It is guaranteed to have improving fundamentals because the market is efficient
It can only happen after a positive earnings report has been released
quiz.explanation: The example shows that price action and business fundamentals can diverge. A new high on the chart says nothing on its own about whether margins or debt are actually improving.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson say a chart 'without business understanding becomes guesswork' for a long-term investor?
options: Because price patterns describe past trading behavior but not whether the underlying business is fundamentally sound
Because charts are only accurate for stocks trading above $100 per share
Because technical analysis was designed exclusively for short-term day traders and cannot apply to anyone else
quiz.explanation: Technical analysis describes price and sentiment, not business quality. For a long-term holder, not understanding the business behind the chart means decisions are based on incomplete information.

quiz.question: Sam notices a stock forming a bullish chart pattern and buys immediately without reading any recent news or financial reports. What does this lesson suggest Sam skipped?
options: Verifying the fundamental thesis, such as revenue, margins, and cash flow, before treating the chart as a buy signal
Checking whether the chart pattern has a official, government-approved name
Waiting for a second identical pattern to form before acting
quiz.explanation: The lesson's action step is explicit: explain the business and profit sources first, then look at the chart. Sam reversed that order, treating a technical signal as sufficient justification on its own.

quiz.question: Based on the lesson's warning, what should a technical signal never be used as?
options: An excuse to buy a company you do not understand
A secondary confirmation after fundamental research is complete
A tool for thinking about position size and risk control
quiz.explanation: The warning specifically targets using technicals as a substitute for understanding the business, not as a tool that can support timing decisions once the fundamentals are already understood.

etfBridge.title: Compare the stock with an ETF
etfBridge.text: If you cannot explain why one company is better than a broad ETF, an index fund may be the simpler and more honest choice.
etfBridge.details: Every single stock has to answer a simple question: why take company-specific risk when you can buy a basket? An ETF does not make you lazy. It gives you a baseline alternative that your idea must beat. If the expected advantage is unclear and the risk is higher, a broad index may be the better decision. Practical anchor: A broad ETF may hold hundreds of companies for one fee such as 0.03-0.20% per year.
etfBridge.example: If you want one technology company, compare it with a technology ETF or the S&P 500. Is revenue growing faster? Are margins better? Is valuation reasonable? Does everything depend on one product? If the answer is vague, an ETF may be calmer. Practical anchor: A broad ETF may hold hundreds of companies for one fee such as 0.03-0.20% per year.
etfBridge.caseStudy: Sofia is choosing between one semiconductor company and a sector ETF. The company may rise more, but it depends on a few large customers and the capital-spending cycle. The ETF may be less explosive, but spreads the risk across many businesses. The choice is about risk-adjusted advantage, not excitement. Decision check: Buying only single stocks too early can make learning mistakes expensive.
etfBridge.action: Compare the company with its sector ETF or the S&P 500 on growth, margins, valuation, and risks.
etfBridge.checkOne: There is a clear reason why this company may beat the basket.
etfBridge.checkTwo: You compared concentration risk, not only past returns.
etfBridge.checkThree: If the edge is unclear, the ETF is treated as a valid alternative.
etfBridge.warning: Do not buy one stock only because it feels more exciting than an ETF. More exciting does not always mean better.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: ETFs can provide diversification while you learn individual stock analysis.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: ETFs can provide diversification while you learn individual stock analysis. The practical anchor is: A broad ETF may hold hundreds of companies for one fee such as 0.03-0.20% per year. The main trap is: Buying only single stocks too early can make learning mistakes expensive.

quiz.question: According to the lesson's practical anchor, what kind of annual fee might a broad ETF charge while holding hundreds of companies?
options: Something in the range of 0.03-0.20% per year
Around 2-3% per year
A flat $500 per year regardless of account size
quiz.explanation: The lesson cites this low fee range as a key reason ETFs can be an efficient baseline: broad diversification for a very small annual cost.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson say an ETF 'gives you a baseline alternative that your idea must beat'?
options: Because if a single stock's expected advantage over the basket is unclear while its risk is higher, the index may be the better decision
Because ETFs always outperform individual stocks over any holding period
Because regulations require investors to own an ETF before buying individual stocks
quiz.explanation: The ETF functions as a reasonable default. Choosing a single stock instead should require a clear, evidence-based reason to expect it to outperform that default on a risk-adjusted basis.

quiz.question: Marcus wants to buy a single retail stock because he 'likes the brand,' without comparing it to a retail sector ETF on growth, margins, or valuation. What step is he skipping, per this lesson?
options: Comparing the company against its sector ETF or the S&P 500 to see if there's a real, evidence-based edge
Calling the company's investor relations department for permission to buy
Waiting for the ETF's price to drop before making any comparison
quiz.explanation: The lesson's action step calls for comparing the single stock to a relevant ETF on growth, margins, valuation, and risk. Liking a brand is not a substitute for that comparison.

quiz.question: Per the lesson's warning, what is not always true even if a single stock 'feels more exciting' than an ETF?
options: That it is automatically the better investment choice
That it pays a dividend
That it has lower fees than the ETF
quiz.explanation: The warning directly states that more exciting does not always mean better, pushing back against choosing a stock based on emotional appeal rather than a measured comparison.

qualityCompany.title: Look for a quality business
qualityCompany.text: A strong company has an understandable product, competitive advantage, healthy margins, capital discipline, and management that does not destroy trust.
qualityCompany.details: Business quality is not found in a glossy presentation. It shows up in repeatable signs: the company earns money, keeps customers, has an edge over competitors, does not burn capital without a plan, and can survive weaker cycles. For beginners, an understandable business with stable economics is usually better than a complex story where success depends on one future breakthrough. Practical anchor: Look for several years of revenue growth, positive free cash flow, and manageable debt.
qualityCompany.example: A quality business may have a product people buy repeatedly, a strong brand, network effects, lower costs, or high switching costs. But each advantage needs evidence in the numbers: margins, revenue retention, cash flow, and competitor behavior. Practical anchor: Look for several years of revenue growth, positive free cash flow, and manageable debt.
qualityCompany.caseStudy: A popular brand is not automatically a quality investment. If sales grow only because marketing expenses explode and margins fall, the brand may be weaker than it looks. Quality appears when the business can earn without endlessly burning money. Decision check: A famous brand is not enough if the economics are deteriorating.
qualityCompany.action: Describe the company's advantage in one sentence and find at least two numbers that support it.
qualityCompany.checkOne: The advantage is clear and not just the company is popular.
qualityCompany.checkTwo: Margins, cash flow, or customer retention support the quality claim.
qualityCompany.checkThree: Management is not diluting shareholders or buying growth at any price.
qualityCompany.warning: If the advantage sounds like an ad slogan and does not appear in the financials, it may be a story rather than an edge.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: A quality company has durable demand, margins, cash flow, and execution.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: A quality company has durable demand, margins, cash flow, and execution. The practical anchor is: Look for several years of revenue growth, positive free cash flow, and manageable debt. The main trap is: A famous brand is not enough if the economics are deteriorating.

quiz.question: According to the lesson's practical anchor, what three things should you look for as signs of business quality?
options: Several years of revenue growth, positive free cash flow, and manageable debt
A rising stock price over the last month and high trading volume
A large advertising budget and a trending social media presence
quiz.explanation: These are the concrete, numbers-based signs of quality the lesson highlights, in contrast to surface impressions like brand popularity or recent price action.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson say a popular brand is 'not automatically a quality investment' in its case study?
options: Because if sales only grow due to exploding marketing spend while margins fall, the underlying economics may actually be weakening
Because popular brands are required by law to disclose lower profit margins
Because brand recognition has no correlation whatsoever with business performance
quiz.explanation: The case study shows that revenue growth funded by heavy marketing spend, while margins shrink, can mask weakening economics behind a still-recognizable brand.

quiz.question: Aisha is considering a company with a beloved logo and loyal social media following, but its margins have shrunk every year for the last three years. Based on this lesson, what should she check before investing?
options: Whether the declining margins point to a fading competitive edge, even though brand loyalty seems strong
Whether the company's logo has been redesigned recently
Whether the social media following is large enough compared to competitors
quiz.explanation: The lesson's decision check warns that a famous brand isn't enough if the economics are deteriorating. Shrinking margins are a numbers-based red flag that brand popularity alone cannot offset.

quiz.question: Per the lesson's warning, what should you suspect if a company's competitive advantage 'sounds like an ad slogan' but doesn't show up in the financials?
options: It may be a story rather than a real edge
It is likely a sign of an upcoming stock split
It means the company is undervalued by the market
quiz.explanation: The warning draws a direct line between marketing language and substance: if the claimed advantage cannot be verified in margins, retention, or cash flow, it is likely narrative rather than fact.

profile.title: Read the company profile
profile.text: Start with sector, geography, revenue sources, customers, and dependencies. If the business is unclear, the numbers are easy to misread.
profile.details: The company profile is the first filter. You should know what the company sells, where it operates, who the customers are, what demand depends on, and what regulatory or currency risks exist. Without that, even good numbers can mislead you: revenue may grow because of a one-off factor, margins may rise because of temporary cost cuts, and profit may improve because of accounting noise. Practical anchor: Before buying, name the segment that drives most revenue and its main customer group.
profile.example: A company may look like a device maker while a major part of profit comes from services. Another may be called fintech but depend heavily on interest rates and credit losses. The profile tells you which metrics actually matter. Practical anchor: Before buying, name the segment that drives most revenue and its main customer group.
profile.caseStudy: Netflix and an industrial equipment maker can both show the same revenue growth, but the business drivers are different. One depends on subscribers, churn, content costs, and attention. The other depends on order cycles, customer capex, and backlog. The same growth number does not mean the same analysis. Decision check: Buying a ticker without understanding the business model is speculation.
profile.action: Write how the company makes money, who the customer is, and the three factors that most affect results.
profile.checkOne: You know which products or segments generate most revenue.
profile.checkTwo: Main dependencies are identified: rates, commodities, regulation, cycle, customers, or currency.
profile.checkThree: You know which metric matters more than headline revenue for this business.
profile.warning: Do not buy a business you cannot explain in plain language to someone outside the market.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: A company profile explains how the business actually makes money.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: A company profile explains how the business actually makes money. The practical anchor is: Before buying, name the segment that drives most revenue and its main customer group. The main trap is: Buying a ticker without understanding the business model is speculation.

quiz.question: In the case study, what is the key difference between Netflix and an industrial equipment maker, despite both showing the same revenue growth number?
options: One depends on subscribers, churn, and content costs, while the other depends on order cycles, customer capex, and backlog
One is allowed to report revenue in a different currency than the other
One is a much older company, which automatically makes its growth more reliable
quiz.explanation: The case study shows that an identical growth number can come from very different business drivers. Understanding those drivers is necessary to judge whether growth is high quality or fragile.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson warn that 'even good numbers can mislead you' without understanding the company profile?
options: Because revenue may grow from a one-off factor, or margins may rise from temporary cost cuts that won't repeat
Because all financial statements are required to round numbers up by 5%
Because good numbers always indicate an accounting error that needs correcting
quiz.explanation: Numbers without context can hide temporary or unsustainable causes, such as a one-off revenue boost or short-lived cost cutting, that make results look stronger than the ongoing business truly is.

quiz.question: Jordan buys shares in a company labeled 'fintech' without checking what drives its revenue, only to discover later that its profits are highly sensitive to interest rate changes. What step did Jordan skip?
options: Identifying the company's main dependencies, such as rates, before forming an investment thesis
Reading the company's mission statement on its homepage
Confirming the company's stock ticker symbol matches its legal name
quiz.explanation: The lesson's checkTwo asks investors to identify main dependencies like rates, commodities, regulation, or currency. Jordan invested without first understanding what actually drives the company's results.

quiz.question: Based on the lesson's warning, what should you avoid doing when considering a stock purchase?
options: Buying a business you cannot explain in plain language to someone outside the market
Buying a business that operates in more than one country
Buying a business that has reported one weak quarter in the last five years
quiz.explanation: The warning is about basic comprehension: if you can't describe in plain terms how the company makes money, you don't yet have the understanding needed to make an informed decision.

statements.title: Review the statements
statements.text: Revenue shows scale, profit shows efficiency, cash flow shows real money power, and the balance sheet shows resilience and debt load.
statements.details: Financial statements should be read as the story of the business. The income statement shows revenue, costs, and profitability. Cash flow shows whether profit turns into real money. The balance sheet shows debt, liquidity, and resilience. One number is rarely enough; the trend over several years and the connection between metrics matter. Practical anchor: Revenue, net income, assets, liabilities, and cash flow should tell a consistent story.
statements.example: If revenue grows but net income falls, look for the reason: margins, costs, competition, or one-off charges. If profit exists but free cash flow is weak, the business may need heavy capital spending or have working-capital problems. Practical anchor: Revenue, net income, assets, liabilities, and cash flow should tell a consistent story.
statements.caseStudy: A company reports record profit, but free cash flow is close to zero. Money may be stuck in inventory or receivables, or the business may need heavy equipment spending. Profit exists on paper, but owners should care about cash left after maintaining and growing the business. Decision check: Reading only one headline number can hide weak cash conversion or rising debt.
statements.action: Check 5 years of revenue, net income, free cash flow, debt, and margins.
statements.checkOne: Revenue, profit, and cash flow are reviewed together.
statements.checkTwo: Debt is compared with profit, cash flow, and rates.
statements.checkThree: One-off events are separated from normal operations.
statements.warning: Do not build a thesis from one quarter. A quarter can be noise rather than a new trend.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: The three financial statements show performance from different angles.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: The three financial statements show performance from different angles. The practical anchor is: Revenue, net income, assets, liabilities, and cash flow should tell a consistent story. The main trap is: Reading only one headline number can hide weak cash conversion or rising debt.

quiz.question: Which practical check fits this lesson best?
options: Revenue, net income, assets, liabilities, and cash flow should tell a consistent story.
Assume price movement alone explains the whole investment.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: The three financial statements show performance from different angles. The practical anchor is: Revenue, net income, assets, liabilities, and cash flow should tell a consistent story. The main trap is: Reading only one headline number can hide weak cash conversion or rising debt.

options: Reading only one headline number can hide weak cash conversion or rising debt.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Assume price movement alone explains the whole investment.
quiz.explanation: The three financial statements show performance from different angles. The practical anchor is: Revenue, net income, assets, liabilities, and cash flow should tell a consistent story. The main trap is: Reading only one headline number can hide weak cash conversion or rising debt.

quiz.question: In the case study, what does it mean when a company reports record profit but free cash flow is close to zero?
options: Money may be stuck in inventory or receivables, or the business may need heavy equipment spending
The company is guaranteed to be committing accounting fraud
The company's profit figure must be incorrectly reported and should be ignored
quiz.explanation: The case study explains this gap as a sign that profit on paper hasn't yet converted into available cash, often due to working-capital needs or heavy capital spending, not necessarily fraud.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson recommend looking at the trend over several years and the connection between metrics, rather than one number alone?
options: Because one number rarely tells the full story; the relationship between metrics like revenue, profit, and cash flow over time reveals more
Because companies are only required to report accurate numbers every five years
Because a single year of data is always inflated due to seasonal effects
quiz.explanation: The lesson emphasizes that a snapshot can mislead, while a multi-year trend combined with cross-checking metrics against each other gives a much more reliable picture of the business.

quiz.question: Felix sees that a company's revenue grew 15% this year and decides to invest immediately without checking net income or cash flow. What is he missing, according to this lesson?
options: Whether the revenue growth is reflected in net income and cash flow, since revenue alone doesn't reveal margin or cash conversion issues
Whether the company's stock price moved up or down on the day of the earnings report
Whether 15% growth is higher than the growth rate from two years ago
quiz.explanation: The lesson's example specifically warns that revenue growing while net income falls is a signal worth investigating. Felix is looking at only one part of the consistent story the statements should tell together.

quiz.question: Per the lesson's warning, why shouldn't you build an investment thesis from a single quarter's results?
options: Because a single quarter can be noise rather than the start of a new trend
Because quarterly reports are not legally binding and can be revised at any time
Because quarterly numbers always understate true annual performance
quiz.explanation: One quarter can be influenced by short-term factors that don't represent the ongoing trend. The lesson advises looking at multiple years rather than overreacting to one data point.

balanceSheet.title: Break down the balance sheet
balanceSheet.text: The balance sheet shows what a company owns, what it owes, and how much resilience it has before a difficult period.
balanceSheet.details: The balance sheet is often ignored because it is less exciting than revenue or earnings. But it shows debt, cash, inventory, receivables, liabilities, and equity. A company can report attractive profit while having a weak balance sheet: too much short-term debt, too little cash, stuck inventory, or customers that pay slowly. For investors, the balance sheet asks whether the business can survive a bad year without panic moves. Practical anchor: Compare cash and current assets with debt and near-term liabilities.
balanceSheet.example: Two companies have the same profit. One has plenty of cash and little debt. The other has large debt that must be refinanced at higher rates. In normal times they may look similar, but in stress the second company has much less freedom. Practical anchor: Compare cash and current assets with debt and near-term liabilities.
balanceSheet.caseStudy: A retailer grows quickly and opens stores, but inventory and short-term liabilities rise faster than sales. Growth may be lower quality than it looks: cash is stuck in goods, while suppliers need to be paid before customers bring enough cash back. Decision check: High leverage can turn a small earnings miss into a serious problem.
balanceSheet.action: Review cash, total debt, short-term liabilities, inventory, and receivables over several years.
balanceSheet.checkOne: Debt is understood by size, maturity, and cost.
balanceSheet.checkTwo: Cash and liquidity are enough for a weaker period.
balanceSheet.checkThree: Inventory and receivables are not growing much faster than revenue without a reason.
balanceSheet.warning: Strong earnings with a weak balance sheet can become a problem quickly when rates rise or sales fall.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: The balance sheet shows financial resilience and obligations.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: The balance sheet shows financial resilience and obligations. The practical anchor is: Compare cash and current assets with debt and near-term liabilities. The main trap is: High leverage can turn a small earnings miss into a serious problem.

quiz.question: Which practical check fits this lesson best?
options: Compare cash and current assets with debt and near-term liabilities.
Assume price movement alone explains the whole investment.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: The balance sheet shows financial resilience and obligations. The practical anchor is: Compare cash and current assets with debt and near-term liabilities. The main trap is: High leverage can turn a small earnings miss into a serious problem.

quiz.question: What mistake should a beginner avoid here?
options: High leverage can turn a small earnings miss into a serious problem.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Assume price movement alone explains the whole investment.
quiz.explanation: The balance sheet shows financial resilience and obligations. The practical anchor is: Compare cash and current assets with debt and near-term liabilities. The main trap is: High leverage can turn a small earnings miss into a serious problem.

quiz.question: In the example comparing two companies with the same profit, what is the key difference between them?
options: One has plenty of cash and little debt, while the other has large debt that must be refinanced at higher rates
One pays a dividend while the other does not
One is publicly traded while the other is privately held
quiz.explanation: Despite identical profit figures, their balance sheets create very different risk profiles. The company with heavy refinancing needs has far less flexibility if conditions become difficult.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson say the balance sheet asks 'whether the business can survive a bad year without panic moves'?
options: Because it reveals debt, cash, and liquidity levels that determine how much cushion a company has during stress
Because the balance sheet is updated daily, unlike the income statement
Because only companies with a balance sheet are eligible to be publicly traded
quiz.explanation: The balance sheet shows the resources and obligations that determine resilience. A company with strong liquidity and low debt has more room to absorb a difficult year than one stretched thin.

quiz.question: Greta is excited about a fast-growing retailer that's opening new stores every month, but she notices its short-term liabilities are climbing faster than its sales. Based on the case study in this lesson, what should concern her?
options: That growth may be lower quality than it looks, since cash could be stuck in inventory while suppliers need to be paid
That opening new stores always reduces a retailer's risk profile
That faster-growing short-term liabilities are simply a sign of strong supplier relationships
quiz.explanation: This mirrors the lesson's retailer case study exactly: liabilities and inventory growing faster than sales can signal that growth isn't being matched by healthy cash conversion.

quiz.question: Per the lesson's warning, what can happen when strong earnings are paired with a weak balance sheet?
options: It can become a serious problem quickly when rates rise or sales fall
Nothing, because earnings are always a more reliable indicator than the balance sheet
The company is required to issue new shares to fix the imbalance
quiz.explanation: The warning is clear that strong current earnings can mask fragility. If rates rise or sales soften, a weak balance sheet can turn a manageable situation into a real crisis quickly.

cashFlow.title: Understand cash flow
cashFlow.text: Cash flow shows whether accounting profit turns into real money that can be reinvested, returned to shareholders, or used to protect the business.
cashFlow.details: Profit can look good on paper, but companies live on cash. Operating cash flow shows what the business generates from operations. Capital expenditures show what must be invested to maintain or grow the business. Free cash flow is what remains after important spending. It often shows the real strength of a business for shareholders. Practical anchor: A company earning $100 million but burning cash needs a stronger explanation.
cashFlow.example: A company may earn $1B in net income, but if it needs $900M of equipment spending every year, financial freedom is limited. Another company with the same profit and low capital needs may have much stronger free cash flow. Practical anchor: A company earning $100 million but burning cash needs a stronger explanation.
cashFlow.caseStudy: A SaaS company may show accounting losses while producing decent operating cash flow. A manufacturer may show profit but constantly reinvest huge amounts into factories. Each case needs context instead of one-line judgment. Decision check: Ignoring free cash flow can make an expensive business look cheap.
cashFlow.action: Compare net income, operating cash flow, capex, and free cash flow over at least 5 years.
cashFlow.checkOne: Free cash flow is not positive only by accident for one year.
cashFlow.checkTwo: Capex is understood as maintenance or growth spending.
cashFlow.checkThree: Cash flow does not diverge from profit without explanation.
cashFlow.warning: Do not confuse a profitable company with a company that truly generates free cash.
