quiz.question: In the example, what happens to financial freedom when a company earns $1B in net income but needs $900M of equipment spending every year?
options: It is limited, because most of the profit must be reinvested into the business just to maintain it
It increases, since high capex signals strong future growth regardless of cost
It is unaffected, since net income is the only number that matters for shareholders
quiz.explanation: Heavy capital expenditure requirements eat into the cash actually available to shareholders, even when net income looks strong, which is why the lesson treats free cash flow as more revealing.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson say free cash flow 'often shows the real strength of a business for shareholders' better than net income alone?
options: Because free cash flow accounts for necessary spending, showing what's actually left over after the business sustains and grows itself
Because free cash flow is calculated using a stricter accounting standard required by regulators
Because net income always includes one-time, non-recurring gains that distort the picture
quiz.explanation: Free cash flow nets out capital spending needs, revealing what cash truly remains for shareholders rather than just an accounting profit figure that may not reflect real spending requirements.

quiz.question: Hannah is evaluating a manufacturer with consistently positive net income, but learns it constantly reinvests huge sums into new factories every year. Based on this lesson, what should she check next?
options: Whether free cash flow remains healthy after accounting for that heavy reinvestment, not just whether net income is positive
Whether the factories are insured against natural disasters
Whether the manufacturer's net income is reported in the same currency as its competitors
quiz.explanation: The case study draws this exact distinction: a manufacturer that constantly reinvests heavily needs its free cash flow checked separately from net income, since the two can tell very different stories.

quiz.question: Per the lesson's warning, what mistake can investors make by ignoring free cash flow?
options: They might confuse a profitable company with one that truly generates free cash
They might overestimate how much debt the company carries
They might mistakenly assume the company pays no taxes
quiz.explanation: The warning draws a direct line between profit on paper and actual cash generation. These are not the same thing, and confusing them can lead to overpaying for a business that doesn't generate much real cash.

profitability.title: Judge margins and efficiency
profitability.text: Margins, ROE, ROA, and operating efficiency show how well the business turns revenue, assets, and capital into profit.
profitability.details: Revenue without margin can be an expensive way to look large. Gross profit shows product economics, operating margin shows expense discipline, net margin shows the result after all costs, ROE shows capital efficiency, and ROA shows asset efficiency. Normal levels differ by sector, so compare margins with the company's history and peers. Practical anchor: Track gross margin, operating margin, net margin, ROE, and ROIC over several years.
profitability.example: A company can grow sales 30%, but if margin falls from 20% to 5%, shareholders should ask whether this is investment in the future or buying revenue too expensively. Growth without economics does not always create value. Practical anchor: Track gross margin, operating margin, net margin, ROE, and ROIC over several years.
profitability.caseStudy: Two companies sell similar products. One has a 25% operating margin, the other 8%. If the first keeps customers and market share, the business model may be stronger. But if the high margin falls every year, the competitive advantage may be fading. Decision check: A one-year margin spike may be temporary rather than a new normal.
profitability.action: Compare gross, operating, and net margins over 5 years, plus ROE/ROA against peers.
profitability.checkOne: Margins are stable or the change has a clear reason.
profitability.checkTwo: Revenue growth is not destroying profitability.
profitability.checkThree: ROE is not high only because of excessive debt.
profitability.warning: High ROE can be a trap if it comes from leverage rather than business quality.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: Profitability measures how efficiently revenue becomes profit.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: Profitability measures how efficiently revenue becomes profit. The practical anchor is: Track gross margin, operating margin, net margin, ROE, and ROIC over several years. The main trap is: A one-year margin spike may be temporary rather than a new normal.

quiz.question: In the example, what happens to a company that grows sales 30% while its margin falls from 20% to 5%?
options: Shareholders should question whether this is smart investment in the future or revenue bought too expensively
The company automatically becomes a better investment because of the higher sales growth
The drop in margin is always temporary and corrects itself within one quarter
quiz.explanation: The lesson explicitly raises this as a question to ask, not a settled conclusion. Strong revenue growth funded by a collapsing margin doesn't necessarily mean the business created more value.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson recommend comparing margins to a company's own history and to its peers, rather than judging them against one universal standard?
options: Because normal margin levels differ by sector, so context from history and peers is needed to judge whether a margin is healthy
Because peer comparison is a legal requirement before publishing investment research
Because comparing margins to history and peers always produces the same result as comparing to a fixed benchmark
quiz.explanation: The lesson notes margins vary significantly between sectors, so judging a margin in isolation, without sector context, can lead to the wrong conclusion about whether it's strong or weak.

quiz.question: Diego sees a company's ROE jump from 12% to 30% in one year and assumes this proves dramatically improved capital efficiency, without checking its debt levels. What might he be missing, per this lesson?
options: Whether the higher ROE comes from added leverage rather than genuine business improvement
Whether the company changed its logo during that same year
Whether ROE is reported quarterly or annually by the company
quiz.explanation: The lesson's warning specifically flags that high ROE can be a trap when it's driven by leverage rather than real operating improvement. Diego should check whether debt increased before celebrating the jump.

quiz.question: Based on the case study comparing two companies with 25% and 8% operating margins, what additional factor should an investor consider beyond the margin level itself?
options: Whether the higher margin is stable over time or has been falling, which could signal a fading competitive advantage
Which of the two companies has a more recognizable logo
Which company has more total employees
quiz.explanation: The case study warns that a high margin that erodes every year may indicate weakening competitive advantage, even though it currently looks stronger than the competitor's lower margin.

valuation.title: Do not ignore valuation
valuation.text: Even a great business can be a poor investment at too high a price. Look at P/E, P/S, margins, growth, and alternatives.
valuation.details: Valuation asks how much you pay for quality and future growth. P/E compares price with profit, P/S compares price with revenue, P/B compares price with book value, and DCF forces you to write explicit assumptions about cash flow. No multiple works alone: a high valuation may be justified by strong growth, but dangerous if expectations are already too optimistic. Practical anchor: Compare P/E, P/S, free cash flow yield, growth, and your base-case scenario.
valuation.example: A company with a P/E of 45 is not automatically bad if it grows quickly, has high margins, and a long runway. But if growth slows and margins are already near a peak, that P/E may mean most good news is already priced in. Practical anchor: Compare P/E, P/S, free cash flow yield, growth, and your base-case scenario.
valuation.caseStudy: Two companies can both trade at P/E 25. One grows 5% with cyclical margins. The other grows 18%, has stable free cash flow, and low debt. The same multiple does not mean the same value. Valuation must be read with quality, growth, and risk. Decision check: A cheap multiple can be a warning if the business is shrinking.
valuation.action: Compare multiples with the company's history, competitors, and your own future growth scenario.
valuation.checkOne: P/E, P/S, or P/B are compared with growth and margins.
valuation.checkTwo: You have base, optimistic, and bad scenarios.
valuation.checkThree: You understand which expectations are already in the current price.
valuation.warning: A cheap-looking stock is not always a bargain. Sometimes a low P/E is a warning sign, not a market gift.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: Valuation connects business quality with the price you pay.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.

quiz.explanation: Valuation connects business quality with the price you pay. The practical anchor is: Compare P/E, P/S, free cash flow yield, growth, and your base-case scenario. The main trap is: A cheap multiple can be a warning if the business is shrinking.

quiz.question: In the example, why might a company with a P/E of 45 not automatically be a bad investment?
options: Because it could be justified by quick growth, high margins, and a long runway ahead
Because a P/E above 40 is mathematically impossible for the market to sustain
Because P/E ratios above 30 are always corrected by stock splits
quiz.explanation: The lesson explains that a high multiple can be reasonable if strong growth and margins justify it. The number alone, without that context, doesn't tell you whether the price is fair.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson say 'no multiple works alone' when evaluating valuation?
options: Because a high valuation may be justified by strong growth but dangerous if expectations are already too optimistic
Because regulators require at least three different valuation multiples to be disclosed together
Because multiples like P/E and P/S are calculated using completely unrelated financial data
quiz.explanation: Multiples need context from growth, margins, and assumptions about the future. A multiple in isolation can't tell you whether the price already reflects an overly optimistic scenario.

quiz.question: Two companies both trade at a P/E of 30. Yara assumes they must carry similar risk and value since the multiple is identical, without checking growth or margins. Based on the case study in this lesson, what is the flaw in her reasoning?
options: The same multiple does not mean the same value, since one company's growth, cash flow, and debt profile could be very different from the other's
P/E ratios cannot legally be identical between two different companies
A P/E of 30 is always considered overvalued regardless of the company
quiz.explanation: This mirrors the lesson's case study directly: an identical P/E can hide very different underlying quality, growth, and risk, so the multiple alone is not sufficient for comparison.

quiz.question: Per the lesson's warning, what might a low P/E sometimes signal, rather than a bargain?
options: A warning sign about the business, not a market gift
Guaranteed strong future earnings growth
That the company has no debt on its balance sheet
quiz.explanation: The warning flips the common assumption that 'cheap' always means a good deal. A low P/E can reflect the market correctly pricing in deteriorating fundamentals rather than mispricing a healthy company.

entry.title: Define an entry rule
entry.text: DCA reduces one-bad-timing risk, buying dips requires discipline, and lump sum does not fit everyone. Write the rule before buying.
entry.details: The entry point should not be an impulse. Different approaches exist: DCA spreads the buy over time, a limit price waits for the valuation you want, buying a dip requires predefined conditions, and lump sum can fit when the horizon is long and the money is not critical for life. The key is to know the rule before emotions start steering the action. Practical anchor: Splitting a planned $1,200 purchase into 3-4 parts can reduce regret and timing pressure.
entry.example: Instead of buying everything today, you can decide: buy 25% now, 25% after earnings, 25% if the price reaches your valuation, and keep 25% for a better opportunity. This does not guarantee the lowest price, but it removes chaotic clicking. Practical anchor: Splitting a planned $1,200 purchase into 3-4 parts can reduce regret and timing pressure.
entry.caseStudy: Nazar wants to buy after a 15% drop. If he does not know why it fell, this may be a trap. If the drop came from the whole market and the business is unchanged, a partial buy can make sense. If it came from lost customers, weaker guidance, or debt stress, the lower price does not make the idea better. Decision check: Going all-in on the first idea leaves little room for mistakes.
entry.action: Choose DCA, a limit price, or another clear entry scenario, plus the condition under which you will not buy.
entry.checkOne: The buy is staged or tied to valuation, not FOMO.
entry.checkTwo: You know what would make you add or stop.
entry.checkThree: The rule for not buying is as clear as the rule for buying.
entry.warning: Do not average down automatically. Add only when the thesis is alive, not because the red number in the portfolio feels uncomfortable.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: Entry strategy controls timing risk and position size.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: Entry strategy controls timing risk and position size. The practical anchor is: Splitting a planned $1,200 purchase into 3-4 parts can reduce regret and timing pressure. The main trap is: Going all-in on the first idea leaves little room for mistakes.

quiz.question: In the example, how is the planned $1,200 purchase split to reduce regret and timing pressure?
options: 25% now, 25% after earnings, 25% if the price reaches a target valuation, and 25% kept for a better opportunity
100% invested immediately to avoid missing any further upside
50% now and 50% exactly one year later, regardless of price or news
quiz.explanation: The lesson's example shows a staged approach tied to specific triggers like earnings and valuation targets, rather than a single all-at-once purchase or an arbitrary time-based split.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson say the key to an entry rule is to know it 'before emotions start steering the action'?
options: Because deciding the approach in advance prevents impulsive decisions once the price is actually moving
Because brokers require entry rules to be submitted in writing before any trade
Because emotional decisions are illegal under securities regulations
quiz.explanation: Pre-deciding your method, whether DCA, a limit price, or staged buying, removes the influence of in-the-moment emotion, which tends to produce impulsive and inconsistent decisions.

quiz.question: After a stock Tara owns drops 15% following a disappointing earnings report showing lost customers and weaker guidance, she considers buying more because 'it's cheaper now.' Based on the case study in this lesson, what should she check first?
options: Whether the drop reflects a worsening of the underlying business, in which case a lower price does not make the idea better
Whether the stock has a recognizable brand name
Whether other investors are also buying the dip at the same time
quiz.explanation: This matches Nazar's case study closely: a drop caused by genuine business deterioration, like lost customers and weak guidance, is different from a drop caused by a broad market move, and the lower price alone doesn't justify buying more.

quiz.question: Per the lesson's warning, when should you add to a losing position by averaging down?
options: Only when the original thesis is still alive, not simply because the red number feels uncomfortable
Whenever the position has fallen more than 20%, regardless of the reason
Only after consulting a financial advisor about the specific stock
quiz.explanation: The warning draws a clear line: adding to a position should be driven by the thesis still holding up, not by emotional discomfort with seeing a loss on the screen.

technicalIndicators.title: Add basic technical indicators
technicalIndicators.text: Technical indicators do not replace business analysis, but they can help show trend, momentum, risk zones, and price behavior.
technicalIndicators.details: For investors, technical analysis should be a supporting layer, not the center of the decision. Moving averages can show trend, RSI can show stretched momentum, volume can show market participation, and support/resistance can mark zones where price reacted before. But an indicator does not know whether the company is expensive, whether earnings are growing, or whether the business model is broken. Practical anchor: Use moving averages, RSI, or support zones as secondary checks after business analysis.
technicalIndicators.example: If a quality company falls to a long-term moving average during a broad selloff, it may be worth deeper research. But if RSI is low because of a bad report and weaker guidance, oversold does not automatically mean attractive. Practical anchor: Use moving averages, RSI, or support zones as secondary checks after business analysis.
technicalIndicators.caseStudy: Anna sees RSI below 30 and assumes the stock must bounce. Then she learns the company lost a major customer. The indicator showed weak momentum, but the reason was fundamental. The better order is: understand the reason for the move, then use indicators for timing. Decision check: A signal can fail quickly when the company thesis or market regime changes.
technicalIndicators.action: Choose 2-3 simple indicators for timing, but connect every buy to a fundamental thesis and valuation.
technicalIndicators.checkOne: The indicator supports entry or risk control, not the reason to own the business.
technicalIndicators.checkTwo: Volume and news context are reviewed, not only one line on a chart.
technicalIndicators.checkThree: A stop or exit scenario exists if the idea is shorter than a long-term investment.
technicalIndicators.warning: More indicators on a chart do not make the decision professional. Often they just create more noise.

quiz.question: What is the main idea of this lesson?
options: Technical indicators are tools for context, not investment theses.
Buy what is popular before checking the numbers.
Ignore the plan if the market feels exciting.
quiz.explanation: Technical indicators are tools for context, not investment theses. The practical anchor is: Use moving averages, RSI, or support zones as secondary checks after business analysis. The main trap is: A signal can fail quickly when the company thesis or market regime changes.

quiz.question: In the example, when might a quality company falling to its long-term moving average during a broad selloff be worth deeper research?
options: When the decline is part of a broad market selloff rather than a company-specific problem
Whenever RSI is below 30, regardless of the reason for the decline
Only after the moving average itself has been recalculated by the exchange
quiz.explanation: The example distinguishes between a broad market-driven dip, which may be a research opportunity for a quality company, and a company-specific decline, which requires more caution.

quiz.question: Why does the lesson say an indicator 'does not know whether the company is expensive' or whether 'earnings are growing'?
options: Because indicators are built purely from price and volume data, not from business fundamentals like earnings or valuation
Because indicators are recalculated only once per year, lagging behind earnings reports
Because indicators only work for companies that pay a dividend
quiz.explanation: Technical indicators are mathematical functions of price and volume. They have no information about a company's earnings, valuation, or balance sheet, which is why they can't replace fundamental analysis.

quiz.question: Leo sees a stock's RSI drop below 30 and immediately buys, assuming it's 'oversold' and due for a bounce, without checking any recent news. Based on Anna's case study in this lesson, what risk is he taking?
options: The low RSI might reflect a real fundamental problem, like a lost major customer, rather than a temporary overreaction
RSI below 30 is mathematically guaranteed to bounce within a week
RSI cannot be calculated correctly for stocks that have recently dropped
quiz.explanation: This mirrors Anna's case study exactly: a low RSI caused by a genuine fundamental deterioration, such as losing a major customer, does not mean the stock is attractively oversold.

quiz.question: Per the lesson's warning, what happens when more indicators are added to a chart?
options: It does not necessarily make the decision more professional and can just create more noise
It always improves the accuracy of entry and exit timing
It automatically reduces the position's overall risk
quiz.explanation: The warning pushes back on the idea that complexity equals quality. Stacking more indicators onto a chart can add conflicting signals and noise rather than genuinely better decision-making.

risks.business: Business risk: revenue, profit, or competitive position can weaken.
risks.valuation: Valuation risk: a good company can produce poor returns if bought at a very high price.
risks.volatility: Volatility risk: stock prices can drop sharply even when the long-term story is intact.

mistakes.storyOnly: Buying only because the story sounds exciting.
mistakes.singleStock: Putting too much money into one company.
mistakes.priceVsValue: Confusing a low share price with a cheap valuation.

fit.growth: You want long-term growth and can wait through market cycles.
fit.ownership: You want to study individual businesses and their economics.
fit.longHorizon: Your goal is far enough away that short-term price swings are less dangerous.

eps.definition: Earnings per share, or profit allocated to each share.

pe.definition: Price divided by earnings. It compares what investors pay with current profits.

dividend.definition: Cash a company pays to shareholders, usually from profits.

marketCap.definition: The total market value of a company: share price multiplied by shares outstanding.

etfs-funds.title: ETFs and funds
etfs-funds.summary: Baskets of securities that let investors buy many holdings through one instrument.
etfs-funds.why: They are often the simplest way to diversify without picking every individual stock or bond.
etfs-funds.intro: An ETF or fund pools many investments into one product. It can track an index, follow a sector, hold bonds, or use an active strategy.

sections.what: Funds bundle holdings. ETFs trade during the day like stocks, while mutual funds usually price once per day.
sections.earn: Returns come from the performance of the underlying holdings, minus fees, plus any distributions.
sections.risks: Funds can still be concentrated, expensive, or poorly matched to your goal. Diversified does not mean loss-proof.
sections.mistakes: Owning many funds can still mean owning the same companies repeatedly.
sections.fit: ETFs and funds may fit simple long-term plans, monthly investing, and broad diversification.

risks.tracking: Tracking risk: the fund may not perfectly follow the index or exposure you expect.
risks.fees: Fee risk: high costs reduce returns year after year.
risks.concentration: Concentration risk: a fund name can sound broad while a few holdings dominate.

mistakes.tooMany: Buying many ETFs without checking overlap.
mistakes.feeBlind: Ignoring expense ratios and trading costs.
mistakes.overlap: Thinking two different fund names guarantee different exposure.

fit.diversification: You want broad exposure through one or a few products.
fit.simplePlan: You prefer rules and consistency over constant stock picking.
fit.monthly: You invest monthly and want an easy DCA target.

expenseRatio.definition: The annual fund cost as a percentage of assets.

indexFund.definition: A fund designed to track a market index instead of picking securities actively.

nav.definition: Net asset value, or the value of the fund holdings minus liabilities.

distribution.definition: Income or gains paid out by a fund to investors.

quiz.question: What is the main benefit of a broad ETF or index fund?
options: Diversification through one instrument
Guaranteed positive return
No fees of any kind
quiz.explanation: A broad fund can hold many securities, reducing dependence on one company.

quiz.question: What does expense ratio mean?
options: The annual fund cost as a percentage of assets
The fund dividend rate
The broker account balance
quiz.explanation: Expense ratio is the recurring cost investors pay for owning the fund.

quiz.question: What is overlap risk?
options: Owning several funds that hold many of the same assets
Owning one diversified fund
Holding cash for emergencies
quiz.explanation: Too many funds can still behave like one concentrated portfolio if their holdings overlap.

quiz.question: Why should an investor compare fees?
options: Small annual fees compound over long horizons
Higher fees always mean better performance
Fees only matter for bonds
quiz.explanation: A fee difference that looks tiny each year can become meaningful over decades.

quiz.question: When can ETFs fit a beginner well?
options: For a simple monthly investing plan
Only for day trading
Only after analyzing 100 companies
quiz.explanation: ETFs can be a practical base while the investor learns individual security analysis.

quiz.question: A user owns five US large-cap ETFs and thinks they are diversified. What should they check?
options: Underlying holdings and sector overlap
Only the fund names
Only yesterday’s price change
quiz.explanation: Fund names can differ while holdings are nearly identical.

crypto.title: Crypto
crypto.summary: Digital assets and networks with prices driven by adoption, liquidity, sentiment, and speculation.
crypto.why: Crypto can offer exposure to new technology, but many assets have no cash flows and extreme downside risk.
crypto.intro: Crypto assets are digital tokens or network assets. Some are used to pay network fees, some claim utility, and many trade mainly on expectations.

sections.what: Crypto is not one asset class with one rulebook. Bitcoin, stablecoins, governance tokens, and NFTs have different mechanics and risks.
sections.earn: Returns usually come from price appreciation, yield programs, staking rewards, or network adoption, but none are guaranteed.
sections.risks: Prices can be extremely volatile. Custody mistakes, exchange failure, scams, smart contract bugs, and regulation can cause permanent losses.
sections.mistakes: Do not treat social media excitement as research. If you cannot explain the asset and custody risk, the position is too large.
sections.fit: Crypto may fit only a limited, high-risk part of a portfolio after emergency savings and core investments are handled.

risks.volatility: Volatility risk: large price drops can happen quickly.
risks.custody: Custody risk: lost keys, hacked wallets, or failed exchanges can mean permanent loss.
risks.cashFlow: Cash-flow risk: many tokens do not produce earnings, so valuation is harder.

mistakes.hype: Buying because a token is trending.
mistakes.keys: Ignoring wallet, exchange, and private-key security.
mistakes.allIn: Using money needed for rent, taxes, or emergency savings.

fit.smallAllocation: You can limit the position to money you can afford to lose.
fit.technologyView: You understand the technology thesis and the specific token mechanics.
fit.riskBudget: Your core plan does not depend on crypto working out.

token.definition: A digital asset issued or tracked on a blockchain network.

wallet.definition: Software or hardware used to control crypto addresses and keys.

exchange.definition: A platform where users buy, sell, or hold crypto assets.

stablecoin.definition: A token designed to track another asset, often the U.S. dollar, with its own reserve and issuer risks.

quiz.question: What makes many crypto assets different from stocks or bonds?
options: They often lack cash flows or ownership claims
They always pay dividends
They are insured like bank deposits
quiz.explanation: Many tokens do not represent business ownership or contractual interest payments.

quiz.question: What is custody risk in crypto?
options: Losing access to keys or relying on a weak platform
A company missing earnings
A bond issuer changing coupon dates
quiz.explanation: Control of keys and platform reliability are central crypto risks.

quiz.question: Which mistake is most dangerous for a beginner?
options: Going all-in because of hype
Using a small predefined risk budget
Learning how wallets work first
quiz.explanation: Crypto volatility can be extreme, so position size matters.

quiz.question: What is a stablecoin supposed to do?
options: Track a reference asset such as a currency
Guarantee profit
Remove all issuer and reserve risk
quiz.explanation: Stablecoins aim to track a peg, but design, reserve, and platform risks still matter.

quiz.question: When might crypto fit a portfolio?
options: As a small speculative allocation within a risk budget
As the emergency fund
As a replacement for all diversified investing
quiz.explanation: A small allocation can express a technology view without threatening the whole plan.

quiz.question: A user cannot explain how they would recover a wallet. What is the best action?
options: Pause and learn custody before buying
Buy first and learn later
Share the seed phrase with friends
quiz.explanation: Operational mistakes can permanently destroy crypto value.

venture.title: Venture and startups
venture.summary: Private company investing where a few winners may drive most returns and many investments can fail.
venture.why: Venture can be powerful but is illiquid, hard to value, and usually unsuitable as a beginner core holding.
venture.intro: Venture investing means funding young private companies before they trade publicly. Outcomes are uncertain, timelines are long, and access is often restricted.

sections.what: Startup investments are private securities. Investors may use equity, SAFEs, convertible notes, or fund vehicles.
sections.earn: Returns usually require a future exit: acquisition, secondary sale, or IPO. Until then, quoted values can be estimates.
sections.risks: Most startups fail or dilute early investors. You may be unable to sell for years, and legal terms can heavily affect outcomes.
sections.mistakes: A great product is not enough. Cap table, terms, runway, market size, and founder execution matter.
sections.fit: Venture may fit experienced investors with high risk tolerance, long lockups, and a diversified approach.

risks.illiquidity: Illiquidity risk: you may not be able to sell when you need cash.
risks.dilution: Dilution risk: later funding rounds can reduce your ownership percentage.
risks.failure: Failure risk: many startups never become profitable or exit.

mistakes.lottery: Treating one startup as a guaranteed lottery ticket.
mistakes.noTerms: Ignoring valuation, liquidation preferences, and investor rights.
mistakes.noDiversification: Making one private investment instead of understanding portfolio math.

fit.highRisk: You can tolerate total loss on individual deals.
fit.longLockup: You do not need the money for many years.
fit.expertise: You have enough domain knowledge to evaluate the company and terms.

runway.definition: How long a company can operate before it needs more funding.

dilution.definition: A reduction in ownership percentage when new shares are issued.

valuationCap.definition: A maximum valuation used to convert some startup financing instruments into equity.

liquidity.definition: How easily an investment can be sold for cash.

quiz.question: What is a core feature of venture investing?
options: High failure risk with rare large winners
Daily liquidity
Guaranteed interest payments
quiz.explanation: Many startups fail, so outcomes are usually uneven and uncertain.

quiz.question: What does dilution mean?
options: Your ownership percentage can shrink after new financing
The company repays your principal
The investment becomes cash overnight
quiz.explanation: New shares or instruments can reduce the investor’s share of future upside.

quiz.question: Why is illiquidity important?
options: You may be unable to sell for years
It guarantees higher return
It removes valuation risk
quiz.explanation: Private investments often lock capital until an exit event.

quiz.question: Which mistake should beginners avoid?
options: Treating one startup as a lottery ticket with too much capital
Reading the terms
Asking about runway
quiz.explanation: Venture risk should be sized carefully because total loss is plausible.

quiz.question: What does runway describe?
options: How long a startup can operate before needing more funding
The stock exchange opening time
A bond maturity schedule
quiz.explanation: Runway links cash, burn rate, and financing risk.

quiz.question: A startup looks exciting but the investor has no terms or exit path. What should happen?
options: Do not invest until terms and risks are clear
Invest because the story is exciting
Ignore dilution risk
quiz.explanation: Private deals require extra clarity because liquidity and information are limited.

cash-risk.title: Cash and risk basics
cash-risk.summary: The safety layer: emergency cash, inflation, time horizon, allocation, and rebalancing.
cash-risk.why: Good investing starts with not being forced to sell risky assets at the wrong time.
cash-risk.intro: Cash and risk basics connect every other topic. Before choosing assets, decide what money is for, when it is needed, and how much uncertainty you can handle.

sections.what: This section is about planning: emergency funds, short-term cash, inflation, risk tolerance, asset allocation, and rebalancing.
sections.earn: Cash protects flexibility. A balanced allocation earns by letting risky assets grow while safer assets support liquidity and discipline.
sections.risks: Too much cash can lose purchasing power to inflation. Too little cash can force panic selling during a downturn.
sections.mistakes: The biggest beginner mistake is investing money that should have stayed safe for near-term needs.
sections.fit: This belongs before every asset decision because it defines how much risk your plan can actually carry.

risks.inflation: Inflation risk: cash can slowly lose purchasing power.
risks.underinvesting: Underinvesting risk: staying too safe for long-term goals can make growth harder.
risks.panic: Behavior risk: no cash buffer can turn a market drop into a forced sale.

mistakes.noEmergency: Skipping an emergency fund before taking market risk.
mistakes.wrongHorizon: Using volatile assets for money needed soon.
mistakes.noRebalance: Letting the portfolio drift far from the intended risk level.

fit.foundation: You are building the base before choosing investments.
fit.shortGoals: You have near-term goals where stability matters more than return.
fit.sleep: You want a plan you can keep during bad markets.

emergencyFund.definition: Cash kept for unexpected expenses so you do not need to sell investments at a bad time.

inflation.definition: A rise in prices that reduces the purchasing power of money.

allocation.definition: How a portfolio is split across asset types such as stocks, bonds, cash, and alternatives.

rebalance.definition: Adjusting a portfolio back toward its target allocation.

quiz.question: What is the job of an emergency fund?
options: Cover required expenses without selling investments
Maximize long-term return
Replace all investing
quiz.explanation: Emergency cash prevents forced selling during personal or market stress.

quiz.question: What is inflation risk for cash?
options: Cash can lose purchasing power over time
Cash always beats stocks
Cash cannot be spent
quiz.explanation: Stable nominal value does not guarantee stable real value.

quiz.question: What does rebalancing do?
options: Returns a portfolio toward its target allocation
Guarantees profit
Eliminates all losses
quiz.explanation: Rebalancing controls drift after markets move.

quiz.question: Which mistake weakens a financial base?
options: Having no emergency reserve
Separating short-term and long-term money
Writing an allocation plan
quiz.explanation: Without a reserve, normal expenses can disrupt long-term investing.

quiz.question: When does cash fit best?
options: Short-term goals, emergencies, and peace of mind
All long-term growth needs
Speculating on volatile assets
quiz.explanation: Cash has a role, but it is not a complete long-term growth strategy.

quiz.question: A user panics and sells stocks after every drop. What lesson applies?
options: Choose an allocation they can actually hold
Increase risk until returns improve
Remove all cash immediately
quiz.explanation: The best allocation is one the investor can maintain through stress.
